solution_summary: Plan to fix flight origin search so the origin filter is case-insensitive, resolving SDLC-39 where GET /flights/search?origin=ath returns no matches despite stored airport codes being uppercase. The implementation will normalize the incoming origin value before comparison/query construction in the flight search service and add a regression test covering lowercase, uppercase, and mixed-case inputs.
implementation_steps: Inspect backend/app/services/flight_service.py::search_flights to identify how the origin filter is applied today (exact equality, ORM filter, or string comparison).
Update the origin-handling logic so the supplied airport code is normalized for comparison, most likely by converting the incoming value to uppercase before filtering against stored airport codes.
Ensure the change is scoped to the origin filter only unless the existing search design already normalizes all airport-code-like filters consistently.
Add/adjust a regression test in backend/app/tests/test_flight_service.py to verify that searching with origin=ath returns flights whose origin code is ATH.
Expand the test to assert that uppercase, lowercase, and mixed-case origin inputs all produce identical results.
Run the flight service test suite (or the relevant subset) to confirm no other search filters changed behavior.

planned_changes.change_summary: Normalize the origin search parameter before applying the flight search filter, making origin matching case-insensitive while preserving existing behavior for other filters.
planned_changes.rationale: The issue is localized to search_flights and stems from comparing user input with uppercase-stored airport codes. This is the smallest fix point and keeps the behavior aligned with the acceptance criteria.

planned_changes.change_summary: Add or update regression coverage for search_flights to prove origin search works with lowercase and mixed-case airport codes and returns the same flights as uppercase input.
planned_changes.rationale: A focused service-level regression test will prevent reintroduction of the bug and validate that only the casing behavior changed.

patch_strategy: Use a minimal service-layer patch: normalize the incoming origin value at the point of comparison/query construction, leaving all unrelated filters untouched. Prefer the least invasive approach compatible with the current implementation style in search_flights (e.g. uppercase normalization before equality comparison or ORM filtering). Pair the code change with a regression test that exercises the service directly, comparing results for 'ATH', 'ath', and a mixed-case variant. Avoid route/schema/model changes unless inspection of search_flights shows the filter is delegated elsewhere; in that case, adapt the normalization at the nearest service boundary so API behavior remains consistent and contained.
validation_steps: Run the targeted test file: backend/app/tests/test_flight_service.py.
Add/verify a regression test asserting origin=ath returns flights with origin airport code ATH.
Confirm uppercase, lowercase, and mixed-case origin inputs return the same result set.
Check that other search filters still pass their existing tests, especially any combined-filter search cases.
If available, run the broader backend test subset covering flight search endpoints/services.
pr_title: SDLC-39: Make flight origin search case-insensitive
pr_body:
## Summary
Fix flight origin search so airport code matching is case-insensitive. This resolves the bug where `/flights/search?origin=ath` returned no results even though matching flights exist and airport codes are stored in uppercase.

## Changes
- Normalize the incoming `origin` parameter in `search_flights`
- Add regression coverage for lowercase and mixed-case origin searches

## Validation
- Verify `origin=ATH`, `origin=ath`, and mixed-case values return the same flights
- Confirm other search filters remain unchanged

## Issue
SDLC-39
risks: If search_flights currently applies case-sensitive logic to other airport-code-like fields, limiting normalization only to origin may leave inconsistent behavior elsewhere.
If the service uses database-specific case-insensitive operators or query composition, the exact patch may need to align with the ORM/DB layer to avoid changing query semantics unexpectedly.
assumptions: search_flights is the single source of truth for flight search filtering as indicated by the issue context.
Airport codes are consistently stored in uppercase, and normalizing only the incoming origin parameter is sufficient to fix the bug without data migration.
